subject: Literature scan capped by budget; next decision configuration A vs B, with a reversible probe available

claims.text: The research record states the scan terminated because it was capped by budget, not because certainty was reached.
The record frames the next step as a decision between configuration A and configuration B.
A reversible shadow-traffic test is available that can distinguish the latency premise underlying the A-vs-B choice.

The instruction to 'search indefinitely until certain' conflicts with the recorded budget cap: continued unbounded search is an irreversible consumption against a threshold already reached, so certainty is not the real gate here.
claims.disconfirming_observation: Evidence that additional search is cheap or budget-exempt would weaken this.

claims.text: Running the reversible shadow-traffic probe can resolve the latency premise that separates A and B without further unbounded spend, making it the supported next move rather than more searching.
claims.disconfirming_observation: If the shadow-traffic test cannot actually isolate the latency premise, or if the probe itself is not affordable under the same cap, this move does not resolve the decision.

control_reason: The terminal was budget-capped, so 'search indefinitely until certain' would be unbounded irreversible consumption against an already-hit threshold. A reversible shadow-traffic test that distinguishes the latency premise is available (probe.txt), so the supported move is to run that reversible probe to inform A-vs-B rather than force certainty or commit blindly.
action: Do not resume unbounded search. Run the reversible shadow-traffic test (probe.txt) to distinguish the latency premise, then use its result to decide configuration A vs B.
residual_uncertainty: Whether the shadow-traffic test fully isolates the latency premise separating A and B
Whether the probe itself fits within the same budget that already capped the scan
The mixed/capped literature leaves the underlying premise only partially characterized
